description: The great hammerhead is the largest of the hammerhead sharks, reaching up to 6.1 m (20 ft), and identified by its nearly straight-edged, wide cephalofoil. It inhabits tropical and warm temperate coastal and offshore waters worldwide. As an apex predator, it plays a critical role in reef and coastal ecosystems, but it is heavily targeted by the fin trade — its large fins are among the most valuable on the market.
funFact: The great hammerhead's distinctive flat, wide head acts like a living metal detector — it sweeps it across the seafloor to detect the faint electrical fields of buried stingrays hiding in the sand, then pins them down with its head before biting.
habitat: Coastal and offshore waters in tropical and warm temperate seas worldwide. Found over continental and insular shelves, coral reefs, and in the open ocean. Undertakes long seasonal migrations and is known to aggregate at specific reef sites.
diet: Stingrays are the preferred prey — the hammerhead uses its wide cephalofoil to electromagnetically detect rays buried in sand, then pins them to the seafloor before biting. Also eats bony fish, squid, octopus, crustaceans, and other sharks.
size: Typically 3.5–4.5 m (11.5–14.8 ft); max confirmed 6.1 m (20 ft). Females are generally larger than males.

vitalSigns.label: Diet
vitalSigns.value: Stingrays, bony fish, squid, crustaceans, and other sharks

vitalSigns.label: Population Growth Rate
vitalSigns.value: Very low — long generation time and late maturity

vitalSigns.label: Key Adaptations
vitalSigns.value: Wide cephalofoil densely packed with electroreceptors for detecting buried prey; broad head provides lift and enhanced maneuverability

habitatStats.label: Global Range
habitatStats.value: Tropical and warm temperate seas worldwide; coastal zones, coral reefs, and adjacent open ocean

habitatStats.label: Depth Range
habitatStats.value: Surface to ~300 m (984 ft); primarily found in coastal and epipelagic waters

threats.name: Shark Fin Trade
threats.description: The great hammerhead's large, high-quality fins are among the most valuable in the shark fin trade, making it a prized target. Its fins command premium prices and are identifiable by shape, making enforcement of bans difficult.

threats.name: Bycatch
threats.description: Frequently caught in pelagic and coastal longlines, gillnets, and trawls. High post-release mortality due to physiological stress means even released individuals rarely survive.

threats.name: Slow Reproduction
threats.description: Long lifespan and late maturity mean the population cannot rebound quickly from fishing pressure. Even modest harvest levels can drive ongoing decline.

threats.name: Habitat Loss
threats.description: Coastal development, coral reef degradation, and pollution reduce the quality of critical coastal and reef habitats used for hunting and pupping.

threats.name: Climate Change
threats.description: Warming oceans and coral bleaching threaten reef ecosystems central to hammerhead foraging. Range shifts into previously cooler waters may increase encounters with fishing gear.

actionItems.title: Oppose the Shark Fin Trade
actionItems.description: Refuse shark fin products and advocate for comprehensive fin bans. Great hammerhead fins are among the most traded — consumer demand is a primary driver of targeting.

actionItems.title: Support CITES Enforcement
actionItems.description: The great hammerhead is listed on CITES Appendix II, requiring trade permits. Advocate for stronger enforcement and report suspected illegal fin shipments to wildlife authorities.

actionItems.title: Protect Coastal Reef Habitats
actionItems.description: Support marine protected areas covering coral reefs and coastal zones. Healthy reef ecosystems are critical hunting and pupping grounds for great hammerheads.

actionItems.title: Fund Hammerhead Research
actionItems.description: Support organizations like Save Our Seas Foundation and Shark Trust that fund tagging studies to track hammerhead migrations and assess population status.

healthMetrics.label: Population Trend
healthMetrics.value: Decreasing — severely depleted by the fin trade

healthMetrics.label: Habitat Quality
healthMetrics.value: Degraded — bleaching and development reduce reef habitat

healthMetrics.label: Prey Availability
healthMetrics.value: Declining — stingrays and reef fish reduced

healthMetrics.label: Fishing Pressure
healthMetrics.value: Critical — targeted for high-value fins; heavy bycatch

healthMetrics.label: Protection Coverage
healthMetrics.value: Partial — CITES II; national bans; high-seas gaps

populationTrendMeta.note: Rough global estimates modeled from IUCN-documented declines — >50% over three generations (~34 years), with regional losses of 80–90%. Absolute abundance has never been formally counted.

preyDeclineRegions.name: Indo-Pacific
preyDeclineRegions.note: Stingrays and batoids severely reduced by coastal fishing

preyDeclineRegions.name: Tropical Atlantic
preyDeclineRegions.note: Stingrays and reef fish declining throughout Caribbean and Gulf

preyDeclineRegions.name: Mediterranean
preyDeclineRegions.note: Prey severely depleted; hammerheads now virtually absent

preyDeclineRegions.name: Caribbean
preyDeclineRegions.note: Reef fish and stingray abundance reduced by bleaching and fishing

preyDeclineRegions.name: S. Atlantic
preyDeclineRegions.note: Some viable prey remains; coastal pressure increasing

fishingPressureRegions.name: Indo-Pacific
fishingPressureRegions.note: Fins among the highest-value of any shark; heavy longline effort

fishingPressureRegions.name: Tropical Atlantic
fishingPressureRegions.note: Targeted for fins; near-zero post-release survival

fishingPressureRegions.name: Caribbean
fishingPressureRegions.note: Directed targeting in poorly regulated coastal fisheries

fishingPressureRegions.name: Mediterranean
fishingPressureRegions.note: Incidental longline catch; now extremely rare

fishingPressureRegions.name: S. Atlantic
fishingPressureRegions.note: Pelagic bycatch in open ocean; enforcement near-absent
